76 - TOP 3 Ways to BUILD Your Sales Confidence - YouTube.mp4

Investors' situation questions— you have to help not only you understand what their real situation is, but more importantly, you're helping them understand themselves what their real situation is. Because most don't understand their real situation when you first start talking to them, right?
How do they respond to that type of question? They just, oh, I start to, I hope I make something, make some money from my 401k, but it's been losing a lot in the stock market and I'm not happy. Like, I know what to ask, and just say it, like, lay back a little bit, pausing, just my tonality. Sometimes I just practice by myself at the nighttime. No, it's really good. You know, Matt, to answer your question there with Gina, I think she made some really valid points. You have to view yourself as like an actor or actress in Hollywood.
Why does Julia Roberts get paid, whatever, $20 million a film compared to somebody else who might even look similar to her that has been in Hollywood for 10 years and is still waitressing tables because they can't get a role? What's the difference? Is it just because Julia Roberts was just magically more talented? Unlikely. It's because she put in a lot more work. Becoming an actress and learning the skills it takes to engage an audience— You know one of the biggest things that they train you in acting school is even how to use your facial expressions to trigger different emotional drivers in their audience's brain. That's why the best actors and actresses, you never turn their shows off because they keep you engaged. How do they keep you engaged? Their tonality?
Their body language, their facial expressions, that triggers emotions in your mind. Can you imagine if Tom Cruise or whoever was on TV and he just talked like this the entire time and his tone sounded the same with everything he said and the questions he asked and he didn't really move his his body around and it just seemed like this the whole time— you would turn off the show after about five minutes because it was so monotone that you lost curiosity. You lost engagement. Okay. So you want to think of yourself as a Hollywood actress. I always say this, you got to, you know, and I'll even pull up the exact quote, because I wrote this down a couple weeks ago and I'm like, I'm never going to, I'm never going to forget what I just said here.
I said this, I said, you have to view yourself. Let me see if I can find it here. You have to view yourself as an actor. or actress always yeah here it is write this down everybody You're an actor. or actress. Always on stage. in a play that never ends. You're an actor or actress always on stage in a play that never ends. What does that mean? That means you're always ready for any sales situation, okay? Because where's the best place to practice tonality? Verbal cues, bridging from question to question using verbal cues. Is it in, like on a sales call or an in-person meeting with a prospect at their business, if you sell B2B or virtual, or at their home if you sell B2C or door-to-door?
Is that the best place on the phone to practice? Or is it best to practice even on people you're talking to in everyday life, asking them different questions, not even that have anything to do with your industry? And being able to use your tonality and using verbal cues to bridge from question to question. See, if you start doing that, if you start practicing on, like, the person cutting your hair or, you know, one of your neighbors, and you get into a conversation and you start using verbal cues to bridge from question to question, where it sounds natural, when you get into a sales situation after 30 days of you doing that. You won't even think about what to do— you'll just instantly know what to do because it's it's in your instincts at this point.
If you don't practice that much, it's like you're trying to figure out what to do on each sales call, and you're going to get crushed. You're going to lose a lot of deals you could be making. So the best place that I learned sales and advanced body language and advanced tonality courses that I went through during my 18-year sales career before I started 7th level was in my car. Driving around. How much time do you waste in your car every single day? Listening to music and your favorite political talk show or whatever you listen to, the news, how much money does that make you? Bagels, zero. It makes you nothing. That doesn't mean you never have to listen to music. Maybe take one or two days on the weekend and listen to music.
But Monday through Friday, you should be in what? Sales mode. Because that's your job, right? I've always got sales training going on in my mind. If you're one of our clients, you should be listening to the 53-hour virtual training course over and over again in your car. You should also be listening to all the archived recordings of the weekly training sessions we have for our clients, like Gina does. And you do that for 30 days, 60 days, 90 days, 120 days, 180 days, one year. It's pretty much impossible not to sell a lot more than you are now, like literally almost impossible. But if you hardly go through any training or you're not even a client, you're like, 'I think I'm going to try to figure it out from watching Jeremy on some free 60-second reels or some basic YouTube videos.
It's going to take you decades to learn what our clients learn in 30 days.' And think of all the sales you're going to lose over the next 10 years trying to figure out on your own, even if you do figure it out, which my background in college was human behavior, social dynamics, and human psychology. That's what I went to school for. It took me 12 years to develop NABQ, and it's still being perfected. So if you're trying to learn from a 60-second reel on, you know, So YouTube or Facebook or IG, it's going to take you 10 to 20 years to try to figure out what our clients learn literally within a few weeks. So, I mean, you've already got this $80,000 work policy. I mean, what's caused you to feel like that's not going to be enough? And then what that question does is it immediately gets them to defend themselves on why they feel what they currently have is not going to get them what they want. See, that's them telling me why they have to change, but more importantly, they're telling themselves. Now, on the annuity side, I could do the same thing. So, I mean, you've got this 401(k) you've been contributing to the last 10 years. I mean, what's caused you to feel like... That's not going to get you enough to be able to retire on.
And then, Gina, what they're going to do is they're going to defend themselves on why what they have, a 401(k), let's say, is not going to be able to get them to retire on time. If that makes sense. Because typically in situation questions in your industry, we're finding out, okay, so if they're talking about retirement, so when are you wanting to... to actually, when do you feel like you need to retire? Oh, you know, I feel like I need to retire by, I'm just going to make up a number, 67. Okay, so you need to retire by 67. When do you really want to retire, though, if you could? Now, don't tell me, you know, you're 44. Don't tell me you're going to retire in a year.
But when do you feel like, if you had the right plan? You'd really want to retire if you could. Well, I'm not really sure. Well, if you really thought about it, oh, I don't know, maybe 61, 62. So why 61, 62 compared to 65? Well, if I retired six years early, I can then... And now they start to tell themselves, on what they would do that extra five years if we're focused on retirement, right? So whatever they have, I can say, so you've been investing in the stock market the last seven years. I mean, what's caused you to feel like? That's not going to be enough to retire on. Or what's causing you to feel like you're not going to be able to retire by 62 with that strategy?
Well, the reason why, and see, now they're telling themselves why what they have will never get them what they want. See what I'm talking about? See, you're still learning. There you go. hours of sales training with me. That's all virtual, but it's recorded, right? So they're little video segments. I mean, Gina, you know, they're five to 10 minutes long, about 50, hours. Now 3. 0, but that's it. There's no group training. There's nobody to role play with that has mastered NEPQ so you get it down for your industry. Now 3. 0 Pro is where you work. You have the portal, the 51-hour portal, but you have group training calls every single week with our sales trainers. I even do one of those a week, that is industry-specific, where I break down each phase of NEPQ. And I show you different industry-specific questions for all the different industries, probably including yours, Keith, because you wouldn't be here if we didn't train your industry. Okay?
So 3. 0 Pro. It's more industry-specific, but that's where you're going to work with our sales trainers on your script. You're going to work with them on your tonality, role playing, which is a big deal because how can you role play like with your spouse, a cousin, another salesperson or company if they haven't mastered an APQ? That's like Michael Jordan taking advice on shooting from a coach who doesn't really shoot that well. Probably not a good thing. You want to be able to take advice from people who have already mastered the thing you're mastering, not somebody that doesn't even know it themselves. So 3. 0 is all the weekly training calls on advanced tonality, advanced body language. the nuances.
Because if I taught all the nuances of every single industry on the planet, how long do you think that virtual training portal would be? You're talking like 30,000 some hours, right? You can't train all that in 50 hours. So you have to have weekly group training questions where you can ask questions to our trainers. There's training on advanced objection handling. So you're bringing the objections you're struggling with. Our trainers show you how to overcome them for industry. That would be the difference. So Keith, just message me and we'll point you in the right direction if you're looking to get in that training program. Okay, Gina, I'm coming back to you. So there's other problem awareness questions we have to ask. Okay, for your industry included. What's a good solution awareness question?
Because as you know, it's not enough to get the prospect to tell you all the problems. That's just half of the equation. We then have to get them to see and feel what the future looks like once the problems are solved. So what's a good solution awareness question we've trained you that really gets them to see what that future looks like? After the consultation, I usually ask them— just want to make sure that I don't make a mistake like all the agents do. So it's just like... um, before we talk today, have you been out there to um, looking for something less similar like this? And I listen to them, depends on the situation. Thank you. And I just...
give them some solution awareness, like, 'What if they don't do?' In the next five to ten years, what? Will be happen with their family or their financial or anything. Yeah, you're talking about the first half of solution awareness. And even in the second half, we're more future pacing, right? So with your industry, because I'm looking at the scripts for your industry, right now. I've got them up here. So I might say, so let's Let's say if they're focused on retirement, let's say with the annuities you're selling and they want like a stable return that protects their principal. Let's just say that. Having that extra five years— What would you do with the extra five years in your retirement?— Then they start telling you. What they would do with those extra years of retirement now that's all logical right, because they're just telling you, 'Oh, I would go on a trip. Where would you go? You know, they talk about Switzerland. You do in Switzerland, right?' I'm getting them to focus on the future, right?
You know, those are questions I'm going to probe and clarify. Then that's so they're giving me logical answers. I get a new car, I do this, that's all logical. Human beings don't make buying decisions on logic, though— they make them on emotion. So then, as you know, you circle around and say, 'Being able to do that, though, What would that do for you?' Personally. Oh my gosh, it would relieve so much stress if I had more time with my grandkids. In what way, though? See, and I'm opening them up emotionally where they're reliving that pain of not having what they want, but they're starting to see what the future is going to look like once those problems are solved from what you're offering. When you ask those questions, how do your prospects respond to you?
They uh they start to uh tell me a lot of things that they never tell me before because they realize that if they don't do this— This is going to happen, but if they decided to move the 401k to... I'm just, you know, this is basically them answering. So if you're getting 60 leads a month, what do you feel is causing you to only get three listings from those? So I'm seeding doubt. That maybe the marketing agency they're using or whatever they're doing for legion doesn't really work that well compared to what we do. Okay, so if you're losing on average four closings a month, times $8,000 each, what's the math on that? And they're going to add it up on their calculator. Okay, so you're... So you're losing...
So you're losing about $32,000 a month in commissions that you should be making if you just had a better lead time. Now, what did I just do there? I'm seeding. Doubt in their mind, and now, what am I doing? Because they added up on the calculator how many listings they're losing because of their leads. Now they see a visual number. For this prospect, they said they're losing $32,000 a month in commissions. So, when you come back and bring up that your marketing agency, let's say, is a monthly retainer of, I don't know, I'm making it up, $4,000 a month or $3,000 a month. Now they're comparing the three grand a month price point to what? Losing $32,000 a month.
You see how I'm making a gap there where now the cost of three or four grand seems pretty cheap instead of losing 32 grand a month. You see what we're doing there? Okay, hopefully that helped you. So, if you're on annuities, right here, so I mean, what is what is your going to retirement going to look like if you just stick with Ameriprise's strategy and you keep having all these ups and downs in the markets?
I mean, how would we be able to retire? Buy 63 with all that. See, I'm getting them to question, right? So with consequence questions, everybody, as you can see in this example, is I'm getting them to defend themselves on what? They feel like they have to change and change now. That builds massive urgency for them to buy now, not keep pushing it down the road. Okay. Now, what they say is what I'm going to plug into that consequence question. As you know, Gina, you're not going to use the same consequence question on every single prospect because they have different problems, right? And you're going to plug those problems in and the consequences if that doesn't change. Now, if they come back, they might, oh, no, I need to do something.
Well, you certainly sound motivated. But for you, why look at doing this now? I mean, why not push it down the road and just hope and pray that somehow the market will just somehow stake in something? Every year. Oh, there's no way the market can stay.
